evals.prompt: I've been implementing the user preferences API (ENG-4521). I got the GET handler and repository done following the existing repository pattern from user_repo.go. The PUT handler still needs to be written, and I need to create the validation middleware — I found that the allowed preference keys should be defined in config/preferences_schema.json but that file doesn't exist yet. The DB migration for the preferences table was done in phase 1 (already committed). I'm on phase 2 of the implementation plan at docs/implementation-plan.md. Create a handoff.
evals.expected_output: A concise handoff document (<150 lines) with YAML frontmatter, task status tracking, learnings about the repository pattern, specific action items for PutPreferences and validation middleware, and file references instead of pasted code
expectations: A handoff markdown file was created in a thoughts/handoffs/ or thoughts/shared/handoffs/ directory
The handoff file has valid YAML frontmatter containing at least date and branch fields
The handoff document is under 200 lines total
The Tasks section indicates ENG-4521 and lists status of GET handler (done), PUT handler (not done), validation middleware (not done)
The document references docs/implementation-plan.md and identifies the current phase (phase 2)
File references use path or path:line syntax rather than pasting full file contents
The document does not contain code blocks longer than 10 lines
The Learnings section mentions the repository pattern discovery (that preferences_repo follows user_repo pattern)
Action items include specific next steps like implementing PutPreferences or creating preferences_schema.json
The response to the user includes the /resume-handoff command with the handoff file path

evals.prompt: I've been debugging the flaky TestConcurrentOrderProcessing test. I found the root cause: OrderHandler.processedOrders is a plain map with no synchronization, and concurrent goroutines cause 'concurrent map writes' panics. I checked the git history and found a previous attempt to fix this with a mutex was reverted because it caused a deadlock in production — the deadlock happened because Dispatch() holds an RLock and the handler tried to Lock on the same mutex chain. I added a DebugListHandlers method to the dispatcher and wrote up my findings in thoughts/debug-notes.md. The real fix needs to either use sync.Map, a channel, or restructure the locking. Create a handoff.
evals.expected_output: A concise handoff capturing the debugging investigation status, root cause analysis including the deadlock history, references to debug-notes.md, and specific next steps for implementing one of the three fix approaches
expectations: A handoff markdown file was created in a thoughts/handoffs/ or thoughts/shared/handoffs/ directory
The handoff file has valid YAML frontmatter containing at least date and branch fields
The handoff document is under 200 lines total
The Tasks section describes the debugging investigation and its status (root cause found, fix not implemented)
The Learnings section captures the deadlock root cause: that the previous mutex fix was reverted because Dispatch() RLock conflicts with handler Lock
The document references thoughts/debug-notes.md as prior investigation notes
The document mentions the three fix options (sync.Map, channel, restructure locking)
Action items specify implementing one of the fix approaches, not just 'continue debugging'
The document does not contain code blocks longer than 10 lines
The response to the user includes the /resume-handoff command with the handoff file path

evals.prompt: I've been working on two things: 1) Applying code review feedback from PR #347 — I finished items 1-3 (error wrapping, cache key constant, context parameter) but still have items 4-5 (cache metrics and product validation). 2) Started implementing cache invalidation — I created pkg/cache/invalidation.go with a basic Invalidate method but still need to add pattern-based invalidation and wire it into UpdateProduct. I discovered the cache uses a write-through pattern. The code review feedback is tracked in docs/code-review-feedback.md. Create a handoff.
evals.expected_output: A concise handoff clearly separating the two work streams (code review + cache invalidation), tracking which review items are done vs pending, noting the write-through pattern discovery, and listing specific action items for both concerns
expectations: A handoff markdown file was created in a thoughts/handoffs/ or thoughts/shared/handoffs/ directory
The handoff file has valid YAML frontmatter containing at least date and branch fields
The handoff document is under 200 lines total
The Tasks section clearly separates the two concerns: code review feedback and cache invalidation feature
The Tasks section tracks which code review items are done (1-3) and which remain (4-5)
The document references docs/code-review-feedback.md
The Learnings section mentions the write-through cache pattern discovery
Action items include both remaining code review items (4-5) and cache invalidation wiring
The document does not contain code blocks longer than 10 lines
The response to the user includes the /resume-handoff command with the handoff file path
